elements.text: 10 new restaurants to try in the new year
RESTAURANTS Continued from Page G1

pork belly bao buns and tacos, miso eggplant, and grilled steak with chimi- churri sauce. Chef Jesus Preciado's menu draws from Asia and Latin Amer- ica (including his native Colombia). On the cocktail list, find drinks like Beet the Heat (made with spicy pisco and beet syrup) and Amarillo Passion (in which tequila meets yuzu liqueur, passion fruit syrup, and the aji amarillo chile).

Roxbury-raised chef Aadam Abdul- Musawwir began serving prepared meals and pop-up dinners as Aadam's Halal Meals. Now, he and two siblings have opened Soul on Shawmut to feed their community. Lightly updated soul food classics are on the menu, along with Buffalo chicken egg rolls, grilled cheese burgers, and more. Fried chick- en with Old Bay honey, smoked mac and cheese, slow-cooked collard greens with sauteed cabbage, sweet potato cheesecake, and hibiscus watermelon juice sounds pretty unbeatable for din- ner tonight.
647 Tremont St., South End, Boston, 617-286-2333, www.desnudacoci- na.com. Smaller plates $6-$18, larger plates $18-$32, cocktails $14-$17.
Kaia

The latest from Xenia Greek Hospi- tality, which brought us Bar Vlaha, Kra- si, and more. This time, culinary direc- tor Brendan Pelley and team whisk din- ers to the Aegean (with a South End mind-set) via dishes like sea bream cru- do with fermented squash broth; barley toasts with beets, pears, and vegan feta; black truffle spanakopita; and grilled whole fish. The wine list is devoted sole- ly to selections from the Aegean and Eastern Mediterranean islands.
722A Sharemut Ave., Roxbury, 617- 238-7525, www.soulonshawmut.com. Sandwiches $10-$18, fried chicken $7-$18, sides $5, desserts $3-$6.

Where others fail, there is Italian food. Standard Italian, in the Eastern Standard complex looming over Fen- way, replaces the former All That Fish + Oyster, and it's a smart move. Lasagna bolognese, porchetta, and espresso martinis could just get us all through the winter. Culinary director Brian Rae is in his element, having previously worked at Coppa, Faccia a Faccia, and Rialto.
370 Harrison Ave., South End, Bos- ton, 617-514-0700, ww.kaias0uth- end.com. Dishes $16-$44.
迎
771 Beacon St., Fenreay, Boston, 857- 305-3095, wrew.standarditalian.com. Appetizers $5-$20, entrees $28-$42, des- serts $9-$12.

Here's what happens when a group of powerhouse South Shore culinary pros (including Jimmy Liang of JP Fuji Group and Kevin Long of Tosca) join forces. This swank Quincy steakhouse is located in a former Masonic temple and serves up lobster bisque, oysters Rocke- feller, pappardelle bolognese, salmon with spiced cauliflower — and, of course, beautiful steaks. The also-beau- tiful, creative desserts are from pastry chef and Masons co-owner Robert Gon- zalez, who is behind Quincy's Niveaux Patisserie. In a landscape (increasingly) stocked with steakhouses, it is nice to see a new one open with local owner- ship.

Butcher Shop, smoky seafood chowder with Irish soda bread, shepherd's pie, bangers and mash, and other pub favor- ites are here, along with wood-fired piz- za, brunch (yes, full Irish breakfast), and festive cocktails. Or just drop in for live music and a proper pint of Guin- ness.

It's named for the small glass of beer that often accompanies pintxos, the Basque take on tapas, and this Beacon Hill restaurant serves up plenty: Gildas, skewers of anchovies, peppers, and ol- ives; griddled shrimp with garlic and chile oil; mini croissants stuffed with ja- mon iberico; squid ink risotto. Cocktails such as the sherry-based Perfect Bam- boo and fizzy glasses of tzakolina are perfect accompaniments. Zurito, locat- ed in the longtime Bin 26 space, is run by chef Jamie Bissonnette, Babak Bina, and Andy Cartin of BCB3 Hospitality, al- so behind Somaek, Temple Records, and Sushi @ Temple Records.
367 Neponset Ave., Dorchester, 617- 514-4689, wrrw.mcgonagles.com. Appe- tizers $8-$16, pizza $18-$19, entrees $18-$34.
JONATHAN WIGGS/GLOBE STAFF
1170 Hancock St., Quincy, 617-689- 8818, wwre.masonssteakhouse.com. Ap- petizers $14-$32, entrees $29-$45, steaks $52-$160, sides $6-$14, desserts $12-$19.

If you're starving, cold, and enjoy wagyu beef, Mikiya is calling you. This Chinatown restaurant serves all-you- can-eat wagyu hot pot at a variety of price points; you have 90 minutes to cook a variety of cuts in the broths of your choosing, while eating specialty dishes such as sweet shrimp sashimi and wagyu bone marrow, plus fruit and ice cream for dessert. Mikiya is a project from Chubby Group, whose Kevin Liu is also behind Matsunori Handroll Bar.
Wagyu sets $45-$98 (less with annu- al membership; free or half-price for kids).
26 Charles St., Beacon Hill, Boston, 857-305-3177, erwre.zuritoboston.com. Pintxos $4.50-$12, raciones $9-$19, platos especiales $17-$125.

If you appreciate the Dubliner, bringing excellent fish and chips and Irish hospitality to downtown Boston, you will ikely feel the same about McG onagle's in Dorchester. Pigs in a blanket made with sausage from Adams Village

Named for the parents of owner Kel- ly Walsh (Sweet Cheeks, Tiger Mama), this Roslindale market offers wine (it's in the former Solera space), olive oil, hot sauce, and other must-haves. But it's the food that has the neighborhood most excited. Start the morning with egg wraps, overnight oats, cheesy grits, and cumin-spiced black beans with fried egg, salsa, and avocado; then segue into the "Cubano, veggie grinder, muffuletta, charred broccoli Caesar" part of the day. Chef Stephen Marcaurelle (the Nautilus Pier 4, Tres Gatos) helped design the menu.
Devra First can be reached at devra.first@globe.com. Follore her @devrafirst.
16 Birch St., Roslindale, 617-833- 6950, wrww.russandmimis.com. Dishes

raised meat and other fresh, healthy ingredients from her Farm at Woods Hill in New Hampshire at this waterfront branch of her West Concord original. Mocktails (most are $8 and up) also showcase ingredi- ents and garnishes from her farm. Sip a Fly-Tai, made with pineapple, orange, lime, almond orgeat, and ginger ale. Good for: entertaining out-of-towners. 300 Pier 4 Blvd., Boston, 617-981- 4577

there are a plethora of base op- tions that can really make a non- alcoholic drink look and taste nearly identical to an alcoholic one," says Joel Atlas, a mixologist at the Avery Bar at the Ritz-Carl- ton, Boston. "The bloom of op- tions has really picked up in the last year and a half to two years."

Stylish, swanky, and with a stellar chef attached (Jody Ad- ams), this Italian destination in- side Raffles Boston is the ulti- mate in special-occasion dining: The silken tagliatelle alone is worth the trip up that grand staircase. Sure, there's also an ex- tensive martini menu, but spirit- free drinks also get proper due. A nonalcoholic tangerine marga- rita with spirit-free tequila and lime is worth toasting. Not quite ready to abstain? There's also an extensive low-ABV list featuring treats like a Lambrusco Cobbler, made with black currant and berries. Good for: a celebratory splurge. 38 Trinity Place, Boston, 617-221-4200

"[Mocktails] are certainly marketed more in January, but the truth of it all is that the non- alcoholic or low-ABV consumer is sustaining through much of the year," says Nick Moniz, gen- eral manager of Banners Kitch- en and Tap and Hub Hall.
Owner Kristin Canty is known for serving pasture-
"For so long, a mocktail was limited to just a soda water and cranberry, or a sugar bomb of juices made with whatever was lying around behind the bar. Consumers wanted and de- served more than that."

Public Garden, and it serves far more than wine. A $10 mocktail menu has spins on classics like ti- ki punch, a cosmopolitan, and a spicy citrus mule. Good for: din- ner with the parents. 272 Boyl- ston St., Boston, 617-279-8000
In that spirit — or lack there- of — here are 12 mocktail desti- nations to try every month of the year

This cheery Roslindale bistro specializes in comfort food: sau- sage dip, mushroom-and-cream- cheese wontons, burgers, and fried pickles. It makes nondrink- ers feel comfy, too, with a nice $12 selection of zero-proof drinks: The Regret Nothing, with nonalcoholic Ritual Proof tequila, jalapeno, and lime, is true to its name. Good for: a laid- back weeknight dinner. 14 Birch St., Roslindale, 617-390-5830
MAH

The storied steakhouse off ers a $14 mocktail menu. Their New Year, New Spritz combines zero- proof sparkling Cava with non- alcoholic tequila, cucumber, lime, and mint for a spa-like pour. Good for: expense-account dining. 793 Boylston St, Boston, 617-536-6300

This South Boston red-sauce lair might seem like the type of spot to throw back icy martinis (they do host Sinatra Sundays, af- ter all) paired with big plates of rigatoni Bolognese, but they also have warming $9 mocktails, like the Far From the Tree, with toasty apple cider, cardamom, allspice, thyme, and egg whites. In addition to catering to non- drinkers, they accommodate a range of palates, with gluten-free pasta on request. Good for: a night out with a group. 443 West Broadway, Boston, 617-993-8080

ioned employs decaf English breakfast tea; a Cucumber Gim- less is an aromatic booze-free gin-and-cucumber palate cleans- er. Good for: sophisticated post- work drinks. 10 Avery St., Bos- ton, 617-574-7176

Sun. 1/12 at 3PM, Sanders Theatre Prokofiev: Quintet in G minor, 0p. 39 Arensky: Piano Trio No. 1 in D minor, 0p. 32 Glazunov: String Quintet in A major, 0p. 39 $30-$68; Sr: $4 of Student: $ 617.349.0086 / bostonchambermusic.org

NOW-FEBRUARY 2 BY LYNN NOTTAGE DIRECTED BY TASIA A. JONES Set in 1950's Brooklyn, two sisters mourn the loss of their mother with Hollywood flms, daydreams, and lots of cookies. When a shocking decision is made by their father, the family must find new meaning in what makes a home. Tickets at lyricstage.com or by calling the box office at
DANCE

In the West End, cozy Alcove does an $11 mocktail menu with unusual offerings like Papi's Punch, with grilled pineapple syrup and Athletic Free Wave nonalcoholic IPA, or a Queen's Bee, made with the herbaceous nonalcoholic spirit Garden 108 and honey. Good for: T.D. Gar- den pre-game hangouts. 50 Love- joy Wharf, Boston, 617-248-0050

This industry hangout takes dive-bar food to another level — they pioneered dressed-up tater tots and mac-and-cheese before it was fashionable. Here, $5 cans of Miller High Life share real es- tate with cheeky mocktails like the Phony Negroni, a nonalco- holic version of the classic, and Island Life, Baby, made with a booze-free, floral Fluère Botanic Blend. Good for: dive-bar-dining without the buzz. 3 Beacon St., Somerville, 617-576-0006

This stylish new Portuguese restaurant in the South End, from the team behind Gufo and SRV, welcomes a cosmopolitan crowd eager to experiment with nonalcoholic delights. Try the Tropical Amargo, an aperitif made with coconut-chai, orange, lime, pineapple, and coffee, plus spirit-free Campari. Good for: date night. 264 East Berkeley St., Boston, 617-505-3243
MUSIC SyIl USICA MarkChurchil, Music Director

Legendary dance company Mark Morris Dance Group performs "The Look of Love"—a must- see homage to the chart-topping hits of Burt Bacharach, including "I Say a Little Prayer," "What the World Needs Now" & more. A powerful fusion of dance and music feat. a live band and vocals sung by Broadway star Marcy Hariell, "this s choreography that, in the words of the song, says 'more than just words could ever say."The New York Times

This is your spot for biscuits, fried chicken, mac-and-cheese … in other words, resolution- breaking meals. That said: This dark, cozy bar and kitchen also pays attention to mocktails, which are a relative steal at $6. Wash down your honey-butter, bacon-topped cornbread with refreshing house-made water- melon juice and soda. Good for: comfort food. 110 Dorchester St, Boston, 617-221-4200

Musgraves Turbulent Landscapes, Elgar Enigma Variations, Wm. Walton Portsmouth Point. Sat Jan 25 7:30pm Hudson High School, Sun Jan 26 3:00pm St. John's Founders Hall Shrewsbury. Tix: https://www.symphonypromusica.org
This might be one of Boston's most classic of cocktail refuges, but it also caters to modern mo- res. A nonalcoholic Old-Fash-
Jan 23–26, Emerson Cutler Majestic Theatre For tix & info: globalartslive.org 617.876.4275
This French restaurant enjoys a scenic perch overlooking the

This Italian mini-empire serves reliable food in conve- nient locations and a thoughtful- ly curated list of spirit-free sips, like a Tea Party with coconut wa- ter, chai tea, and brown sugar, and the Cos-No-Politan, made with honey water, cranberry, rooibos tea, and lime. Beverage director Jose Luis Betancur also has a fondness for tart Italian so- das like Chinotto or Stappi Gas- sosa. Good for: pleasing a crowd (or kids). 64 Seaport Boulevard, Boston, 617-303-7300, and other locations
Soul on Shawmut
Standard Italian
